source_metadata.title_raw: Jean B. Savaree - A-19-035 - March 15, 2019 - San Carlos

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jean B. Savaree 
Woodside Town Attorney 
Office of the Town Attorney 
1001 Laurel Street, Suite A 
San Carlos, CA 94070 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-035 
Dear Ms. Savaree: 
March 15, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Town of Woodside, Mayor 
Daniel Yost, and Planning Commissioner Kurt Cali a regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Mayor Yost, as the owner of a lot in the Woodside Glens (the "Glens"), vote on 
proposed amendments to the Woodside Municipal Code ("WMC") which would apply to the Glens 
neighborhood of the Town? 
2. May Planning Commissioner Calia, as the owner of a lot in the Glens, vote on proposed 
amendments to the WMC which would apply to the Glens neighborhood of the Town? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-035 
Page No. 2 
1 & 2. Since Mayor Yost and Planning Commissioner Calia's respective properties will be 
subject to the amended development standards for the Glens neighborhood that would be 
encompassed within the proposed changes to the WMC, they would both have a conflict of interest 
and may not make, participate in making, or use their official positions to influence a governmental 
decision regarding those changes. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
The Town of Woodside is a general law city located in San Mateo County, California. The 
Town is divided into seven separate Districts. Councilmembers are elected from each of the seven 
Districts from votes cast Town wide. Town Councilmembers are required to reside within the 
District from which they were elected. The Planning Commission is comprised of seven members, 
with one member appointed from each District. Planning Commissioners are required to reside 
within the District from which they are appointed. 
The Town's population is 5,287 (2010 US Census). There are a total of2,363 residentially-
zoned parcels, and 27 commercially-zoned parcels in Town. 
The Woodside Town Council has authorized a comprehensive review of the Town's 
development standards as they relate to nonconforming lots, structures, and improvements within 
the Town. The Town Council has directed that the Planning Commission examine the effect of the 
Town's current development standard on a neighborhood by neighborhood basis. The first 
neighborhood to be studied is the Glens. 
The Glens is comprised of 80 acres of land, approximately one-half mile north of the 
Town's commercial area. The Glens is located in District 1. Mayor Yost is the Councilmember 
elected from District 1. Planning Commissioner Calia is the Planning Commissioner appointed 
from District 1. Both Mayor Yost and Planning Commissioner Calia own homes in the Glens 
neighborhood. 
The Glens was originally established through the recordation of two subdivisions, the 
Woodside Glens Map No.1, filed in 1925, and Woodside Glens Map No.2, filed in 1929. The two 
subdivisions created 254 lots, with typical lot sizes of 10,000 to 12,000 square feet, but some are as 
small as 7,000 square feet and a few are 20,000 square feet or more. Over the past 90+ years, homes 
have been constructed on about 185 of the lots. 
The Town of Woodside was incorporated in 1956. "R-1" zoning was applied to the Glens 
area, thereby requiring minimum 20,000 square foot lots. 
There are currently a total of 194 parcels in the Glens neighborhood, and 85% are 
nonconforming in regard to lot size. The Town's current development standards were created for 
flatter, conforming lots. When these standards are applied to the Glens nonconforming lots, 
especially those with steep terrain, these standards create the need for numerous exceptions and/or 
variances. Because this occurs most often on the smallest or least developable lots ( e.g., The Glens, 


File No. A-19-035 
Page No. 3 
or the Emerald Hills neighborhood both designated R-1 ), these standards create a disproportionate 
financial impact on the property owners in these neighborhoods. 
Recognizing that each neighborhood within Woodside is unique, as noted above, the Town 
Council instructed that in reviewing development standards, each neighborhood be studied 
separately. Because the lot nonconformities and constraints in the Glens affect 85% of its 
properties, thereby creating disproportionate financial and safety impacts for those property owners, 
the Town Council instructed that the Town wide review begin in the Glens. 
Initial outreach meetings with Glens residents have occurred and the property owners have 
an interest in exploring revisions to currently established development standards for setbacks, 
height limitations, floor area, main residence size, slope restrictions, parking/driveway 
requirements, paved area and surface coverage limitations, accessory dwelling units regulations, 
nonconforming rights, the impacts of nonconformity, fire requirements, strategies to address 
abandoned homes, entitlements and the planning and building process. 
The public input component of this review will continue into April 2019, at which time staff 
will formulate recommendations regarding potential revisions to the Town's current development 
standards for Planning Commission review. The Planning Commission will be asked to receive 
public input, review the staff recommendations, and to formulate its own independent 
recommendations for the Town Council. The Planning Commission's recommendations will then 
be presented to the Town Council. The Town Council will then hold public hearings and decide 
whether or not to amend the WMC to revise development standards for the Glens neighborhood. 
Changes to the development standards for the Glens could also require amendments to the Glens 
Specific Plan to ensure consistency between the Woodside Zoning Code and the Glens Specific 
Plan. 
It is unknown, at this time, how far reaching any changes to the development standards will 
be (i.e., whether changes will apply only to nonconforming lots or to all lots in the Glens). 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state 
or local government agency, including city councilmembers. 
Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are set forth in Section 87103. 
The interest pertinent to the officials in question is set forth in Section 87103(b ): "An interest in real 
property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more." (Section 
87103(b).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
In order for an interest in real property to be disqualifying, the specific decision in question 
must have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the property. 


File No. A-19-035 
Page No. 4 
Regulation 18701 ( a) provides that a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably 
foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the 
official or the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
Additionally, Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial 
interest is material whenever the governmental decision, "Involves the adoption or amendment of 
development criteria applicable to the parcel." 
Although you indicate that it is unknown, at this time, how far reaching any changes to the 
development standards will be, the process will involve "exploring revisions to currently 
established development standards for setbacks, height limitations, floor area, main residence size, 
slope restrictions, parking/driveway requirements, paved area and surface coverage limitations, 
accessory dwelling units regulations, nonconforming rights, the impacts of nonconformity, fire 
requirements, strategies to address abandoned homes, entitlements and the planning and building 
process." Thus, the proposed actions involve the amendment of development criteria applicable to 
the parcels, and the effect of the decision on the parcels is reasonably foreseeable under Regulation 
18701(a) and material under 18702.2(a)(l). 
Accordingly, Mayor Yost and Planning Commissioner Calia have disqualifying conflict of 
interests and may not take part in the decisions. We have no facts to suggest an exception would 
apply. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
2 The Commission adopted amendments to Regulation 18702.2 at its January 17, 2019 meeting, and this 
analysis applies that regulation as amended.
